prompt: I need to add pagination to a service that currently returns all results. The existing code mixes data fetching and formatting. What should I do before writing any new code?
expected: Skill activates and identifies preparatory refactoring: with the refactoring hat on, first separate the data-fetching and formatting concerns so the code is structured for pagination, verify tests still pass, then switch to the feature hat and add pagination as the now-easy change (make the change easy, then make the easy change).
notes: V2 canonical scenario — preparatory refactoring before adding a feature; source id: ST-01

prompt: I've been reading this legacy function for 20 minutes and I finally understand what it does. How should I proceed?
expected: Skill activates in comprehension mode: rather than holding the understanding in your head, externalize it into the code now — rename the variables/functions whose meaning you decoded and extract the blocks whose purpose you figured out, running tests after each change to move understanding from your head into the code itself.
notes: Comprehension refactoring — understanding should be moved into code, not kept in head; source id: ST-02

prompt: While fixing a bug I noticed the three calling sites all duplicate the same validation logic. Should I fix that now or just patch the bug?
expected: Skill activates in litter-pickup mode and applies size triage: unifying three duplicated validation sites is a small, in-path fix, so consolidate the duplication now rather than only patching the bug — fixing the duplication reduces the chance of similar bugs recurring in the same code.
notes: Litter-pickup triage — small problem noticed in passing, likely worth fixing now; source id: ST-03

prompt: Our team has agreed the dependency graph in the payments module is a mess. We can't freeze the codebase to fix it — how do we approach this?
expected: Skill activates in long-term mode: agree on a target state, then use Branch By Abstraction to introduce a layer that works with both old and new implementations and migrate callers incrementally — no feature freeze or sprint; everyone passing through the module moves it one step toward the target, and CI keeps main green at every step.
notes: Long-term refactoring — large structural problem requiring weeks, Branch By Abstraction; source id: ST-04

prompt: I want to add a new discount type to the pricing engine but the existing discount logic is hardcoded in five different places. What's the right sequence of steps?
expected: Skill activates in preparatory mode: first make the change easy by consolidating/parameterizing the five hardcoded discount sites into a single point of variation (refactoring hat, tests passing), then switch to the feature hat and add the new discount type as a trivial change — avoiding a sixth duplicate.
notes: Preparatory — make the change easy first (consolidate discount logic), then add the new type; source id: ST-05

prompt: Our team just got approval for a refactoring sprint next month. We've been accumulating technical debt for two years. How should we use this time and how do we avoid needing another one?
expected: Skill activates and frames the sprint as a symptom of missing opportunistic refactoring: execute the planned episode (valid for a neglected codebase), but use it to establish the four-mode posture — preparatory, comprehension, litter-pickup, and long-term refactoring built into daily work — so refactoring stops being budgeted separately and no future cleanup sprint is needed.
notes: Planned refactoring as symptom — execute the sprint, but establish opportunistic habits to prevent recurrence; source id: ST-06

prompt: I'm reviewing code that was written six months ago and I'm having trouble following the conditional logic. Should I just leave comments or is there something better?
expected: Skill activates in comprehension mode: rather than only leaving comments, lock the understanding into the code by renaming the unclear identifiers and extracting the confusing conditional logic into well-named functions, verifying with tests — the code becomes its own documentation instead of relying on comments.
notes: Comprehension refactoring during code review — rename and extract, don't just comment; source id: ST-07

prompt: While adding a new endpoint I noticed the existing error handling is inconsistent — some places return 404, others 400, for the same logical error. It would take about 3 hours to fix. What should I do?
expected: Skill activates in litter-pickup mode and applies size triage: a ~3-hour fix is large, so note it (ticket/TODO) and defer rather than derail the current endpoint work — do not abandon the task in progress to pursue a large cleanup; capture it for later or handle via long-term mode.
notes: Litter-pickup with size triage — 3 hours is large, note and defer rather than derail current task; source id: ST-08

prompt: I have a function that's 200 lines long. How do I break it apart?
My manager says we don't have time for refactoring and it doesn't deliver business value. How do I convince them?
What are the code smells I should look for in this class?
I don't have any tests for this module. Should I refactor it to understand it better?
expected: The skill should fire (comprehension mode is triggered) but immediately apply the Boundary constraint: opportunistic refactoring without a fast test suite is unsafe. Advise getting tests in place first (via WELC techniques) before comprehension refactoring. Do not simply recommend refactoring as if tests don't matter.
notes: Boundary condition — no tests means the skill applies conditionally, with explicit safety warning; source id: EC-01

prompt: I'm halfway through adding a new feature and I realize the code structure needs to change to make the rest of the feature easy. Should I refactor now?
expected: Preparatory refactoring mid-feature is valid but requires switching hats explicitly. The skill should recommend: stop feature work, commit what you have, switch to refactoring hat, make the structural change, run tests, then switch back to feature hat. Two Hats discipline applies — don't mix both in one mental state. Note that the refactoring and feature work may need to be in separate commits.
notes: Overlap with Two Hats — preparatory mode applies but requires hat discipline to execute safely; source id: EC-02

prompt: While working on a ticket I found a security vulnerability in nearby code that's not part of my ticket. Should I fix it as litter-pickup?
expected: The skill should recognize this as a litter-pickup signal but escalate the triage: security vulnerabilities are not size-triaged by time cost alone — they have risk dimensions. Recommend fixing immediately if small and isolated, but if larger, raise it as a separate urgent item rather than deferring with a note. The litter-pickup 'note and defer' path is inappropriate for security issues.
notes: Litter-pickup mode with atypical triage criteria — not all deferred notes are equal; source id: EC-03
